narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 41, which exceeds the dataset average of 36.70, you brought some impressive academic credentials to your law school application. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you've indicated plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and bar exam outcomes, predicted a 75.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this model to prioritize applicants likely to pass the bar and succeed in their program. The decision hinged most heavily on your school decile rankings from earlier years, particularly your Year 3 performance, where you ranked in the bottom decile (1 out of 10), far below the average of around the middle of the pack (5.53). This low Year 3 standing was the strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. Your Year 1 decile of 2—again, well below the average 5.71—was the second-biggest influence, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that could carry over to law school rigor and the bar. On a brighter note, your standout LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass, nearly offsetting the impact of those deciles. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early deciles are notably weaker, while your LSAT aligns with top performers, highlighting a mixed but risk-heavy profile in the model's eyes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building a stronger recent academic record to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate programs or targeted prep that demonstrates improvement. You might also gather letters of recommendation emphasizing your LSAT success and resilience as a full-time student from a stable background. With targeted steps to address those early grades, you could reapply stronger next cycle and shift the model's view in your favor.
